retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the basic pathophysiology and toxicokinetics of fluorocarbon exposure toxicity. Identify the common presenting features of fluoride and fluorocarbon toxicity on an examination/evaluation. Outline the treatment and management strategies available for patients with fluoride/fluorocarbon toxicity. Summarize how an optimally functioning interprofessional team would coordinate care to enhance outcomes for patients suffering from fluorocarbon toxicity. retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients recover within 24 hours [18] [1] [4] . In one study, approximately 50% of reported cases required hospital admission [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Basic supportive measures, such as removal of the exposure, fresh air, and supplemental oxygen, are adequate in many cases [1] [4] [2] [13] [8] [16] [15] . Inhaled beta-2 adrenergic agonists and corticosteroids have been used with success in patients with physical exam evidence of bronchospasm and may represent a reasonable intervention in selected cases [15] [16] [17] [2] [4] . Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation and endotracheal intubation have been required in some cases [1] . Treatment with antibiotics should be reserved for patients with clinical findings consistent with respiratory infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Clonidine Toxicity(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: If the ingestion was intentional, then intervention from psychological professionals is necessary. For unintentional ingestion, the patient will require counsel on proper dosing and administration to prevent further events. If the overdose occurred in a child, it is often a case of the patient or parent needing to understand the importance of keeping the drug in a place not accessible by the child.

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although the basics of evaluating and treating most unknown ingestants are well known, many overdoses require very specific treatment if the patient is to have any chance of survival. [2] [3] [4] [5] while each has its own merits, most focus solely on pediatric overdoses, and relatively few of them provide an exhaustive list or act as a "quick-reference" when dealing with real-time ingestions.

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Although the basics of evaluating and treating most unknown ingestants are well known, many overdoses require very specific treatment if the patient is to have any chance of survival. Despite widespread public education, childproof containers, and other safety measures, accidental overdoses continue to occur. In the United States, poison control centers receive over 2.2 million calls each year, 47% of which concern children less than six years of age. Most pediatric accidental ingestions involve cosmetics and personal care products, followed by cleaning products, and then analgesics and prescription medications. Adult overdoses usually are due to the intentional ingestion of analgesics and sedative-hypnotics. This activity reviews the deadly single dose toxic agents and discusses the interprofessional team's role in educating families on protecting children from exposure to these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Primary concern with the high corrosiveness and toxicity of hydrofluoric acid should be through the prevention of exposure.   All individuals that use HF should be aware of the toxic properties of the agent which include information and procedures for safe handling of the acid, appropirate means of transporting and storage, management of waste containing HF, gross and fine decontaminiation procedures and medical treatment for exposure and intoxication.   Hydrofluoric acid should be exclusively used in industrial settings and laboratories that are appropriately equipped to handle the chemical, have appropriate exposure control plans in place which includes having appropriate treatment modalities on site to initiate therapy prior to transport of the exposed individual to the hospital.  Likewise, household and cleaning agents should be stored out of the reach of children.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Issues of Concern -- Professionally Applied topical Fluoride
retrieved_docs.content: Because of the low risk of experiencing harm in children younger than six, 2.26% fluoride varnish is the only topical fluoride agent recommended for this age group, even though other topical fluorides may be beneficial. [15] The application should be made twice per year in primary and permanent dentition. However, in patients with an elevated caries risk, the varnish should be applied every three months. [13]

retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad category of medical conditions that arise as a result of toxic exposure, and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances is recognized as a specific type within this category. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1566
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental nefopam poisoning is a toxic condition that results from exposure to the analgesic drug nefopam, with nefopam identified as the causative agent in these poisoning events. This relationship highlights that overdose or inadvertent administration of nefopam can lead to serious adverse effects, underscoring the importance of recognizing nefopam as the responsible agent for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of accidental toxicity.
